name: Clove

chemicals.name: Clove Essential Oil (Categorized under essential oils known to be toxic to cats)
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Hepatic Enzyme Deficiency: Cats possess a unique metabolic profile that lacks specific liver enzymes (specifically hepatic glucuronosyltransferase) required to break down and glucuronidate certain organic compounds found in essential oils2. ◦ Accumulation and Organ Damage: Because the cat's liver cannot effectively process or eliminate these toxins, they accumulate in the body, leading
chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ High Toxicity: The oil is considered toxic and can be harmful even in small quantities, such as a single drop of concentrated product4. ◦ "Neat" Application: The risk is highest when the oil is used "neat" (undiluted) rather than in low-concentration cosmetic products5. Toxicity can occur through ingestion (licking fur), inhalation, or direct skin contact

treatments.name: Warm Water Bath with Liquid Dish Soap
treatments.description: If the cat has Clove oil on its skin or fur, the veterinarian (or owner, under guidance) must bathe the cat using lukewarm water and a liquid dish soap (degreaser) to thoroughly remove the oily residue. This prevents the cat from absorbing more toxins through the skin or ingesting them while grooming.
treatments.notes: This is urgent because cats are fastidious groomers. Do not use solvents or other cleaning products to remove the oil, as these can be toxic themselves.Oral Decontamination — Do NOT Induce Vomiting

treatments.name: Mouth Rinsing / Dilution
treatments.description: If the oil was ingested, the veterinarian may rinse the cat’s mouth with lukewarm water to remove localized traces of the oil. They may also administer small amounts of liquid (like milk or water) to dilute the substance.
treatments.notes: Inducing vomiting is contraindicated (not recommended) for essential oil ingestion. Clove oil can cause chemical burns to the mouth and esophagus; bringing it back up can worsen this damage or lead to aspiration (inhaling the oil into the lungs), which causes severe pneumonia.Aggressive Supportive C

treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Oxygen Supplementation
treatments.description: The administration of IV fluids to maintain hydration, blood pressure, and assist the body in eliminating toxins. If the cat is experiencing difficulty breathing (a symptom of essential oil poisoning), oxygen therapy may be required.
treatments.notes: Essential oils can cause liver injury, seizures, and respiratory distress. Aggressive care is often needed to support the cat's organs while the liver attempts to process the toxins, a task made difficult by the cat's lack of specific liver enzymes (glucuronosyltransferase).Symptomatic Management (P

treatments.name: Analgesics and Anticonvulsants
treatments.description: Medications may be given to control tremors or seizures (neurological signs of toxicity) and to manage pain from chemical burns in the mouth or throat.
treatments.notes: Cats exposed to essential oils may show signs of CNS depression (dullness, wobbliness) or excitation (tremors). Liver protectants may also be administered if blood tests indicate hepatic damage.

emergencyNote: Warm Water Bath with Liquid Dish Soap
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Drooling, vomiting, lethargy, loss of coordination (ataxia), and muscle tremors from exposure; liver damage signs (jaundice, loss of appetite) with larger exposures; respiratory distress if inhaled.

Essential oil is far more dangerous than whole cloves: Concentrated clove oil can cause serious poisoning with just a few drops. Whole cloves or ground clove in small amounts pose lower but still real risk.
Action: Seek veterinary care immediately if a cat has been exposed to clove essential oil — on the skin, ingested, or via diffuser. Liver damage can progress rapidly.
description:
Clove (Syzygium aromaticum) is a common kitchen spice, but it is not safe for cats, especially in the form of clove essential oil. The main concern is eugenol, a highly concentrated and strongly irritating plant compound found in cloves.

Humans can usually break down these compounds more effectively, but cats have a much more limited ability to process them in the liver. Because of this, eugenol and related phenolic compounds may build up in a cat’s body and cause poisoning. In serious cases, they may even damage the liver.

Clove essential oil is more dangerous than dried cloves because it is highly concentrated and can be absorbed through the skin, mouth, or mucous membranes. Even a small amount of exposure may cause irritation or toxicity. If you have cats at home, avoid using clove essential oil and keep cats away from clove products so they cannot lick, chew, or have prolonged contact with them.

symptoms.name: Liver failure (Serious organ damage)
symptoms.notes: Essential oils can cause serious organ damage in cats; in extreme cases, this leads to liver failure because cats lack the specific liver enzymes needed to break down these compounds.Neurological Disturbances (Tremors and Ataxia)

symptoms.name: Tremors, shaking, and ataxia (walking as if drunk)
symptoms.notes: Cats may also experience collapse or seizures, indicating severe central nervous system toxicity.Respiratory Distress

symptoms.name: Difficulty breathing (Dyspnea) or breathing with the mouth open
symptoms.notes: This can occur from inhalation (diffusers) or aspiration if the cat vomits. Nebulized or ultrasonically diffused neat oils pose a greater risk.Chemical Burns

symptoms.name: Soreness, redness, or chemical burns
symptoms.notes: Direct skin contact with the oil can cause burns. Even if there is no visible damage to the skin, the cat may still be poisoned by absorbing the toxins or licking them off their fur.Gastrointestinal Upset

symptoms.name: Vomiting and excessive dribbling (hypersalivation)
symptoms.notes: Vomiting is a common sign of essential oil poisoning. High fat levels in carrier oils can also contribute to tummy upsets.Lethargy

symptoms.name: Lethargy, depression, or dullness
symptoms.notes: A general sign of systemic toxicity that often accompanies more specific symptoms.
